content.assessment: This proposal is the demographic lens's native terrain: it explicitly models the counterfactual contraction baseline using the strongest evidence set in this lens (F-live-demography-01 through F-live-demography-09 collectively). Its core claim—that cohort decline plus statutory class-size floors mechanically force consolidation independent of policy choice—is well-supported. The proposal correctly identifies that this contraction is already underway (application shifts, capacity stagnation) and is structurally embedded for 20+ years, not a short-term fluctuation. Its own risk analysis appropriately flags the central demographic weakness: passive contraction is geographically uneven, meaning urban/Budapest selective capacity could persist while rural provision collapses, an outcome directly evidenced by the region-specific contraction data and the settlements down to 50-70 school-age children. This makes the proposal analytically strong as a counterfactual baseline, but the lens's own limitation applies squarely: population projections describing what will mechanically happen do not determine whether that outcome (uneven geographic collapse, unaddressed Roma segregation, church-sector growth) is normatively acceptable.
opportunities: Serves as an essential comparison baseline against which the demographic cost-effectiveness of T1-T4 can be measured, since it isolates what happens with zero new policy
strengths: Directly and comprehensively grounds itself in the strongest available cohort-decline evidence (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02), correctly framing decline as structurally embedded rather than transient
Correctly identifies the mechanical class-size threshold (F-live-demography-05) as an independent-of-policy constraint already binding in shrinking localities
Explicitly incorporates the aging/rural-shortage teacher workforce (F-live-demography-09) as a compounding pressure, showing awareness of multiple demographic mechanisms operating simultaneously
Own risk analysis correctly anticipates the geographically uneven outcome (urban persistence vs. rural collapse) that the regional contraction data (F-live-demography-04) would predict
threats: Uncoordinated, unplanned contraction could produce settlement-viability disruptions analogous in kind to the Polish transition-cost pattern, and does nothing to prevent Roma segregation or church-sector growth from persisting or worsening even as aggregate selective-track size shrinks
weaknesses: As a passive baseline, it has no mechanism to manage the uneven spatial distribution it correctly identifies as a risk; contraction will occur but its geographic pattern is left entirely to market/demographic drift
